Previous action: my flop c-bet call, turn check her bet call shows strength/interest; value bet extracts from worse (pairs of 5/2/7/T, Ax), charges missed draws per her calling station history (Hands 20/26/41/43/49/65/71/74/104/106+). Sizing 700 (~2/3 pot) balances value/protection without overbetting OOP, induces calls from medium strength while preserving fold equity if raised (stackoff viable with two pair late tournament). Check risks free showdown or her bet where I can raise/call but reduces value realization.

Elon limped preflop, made a small c-bet on the flop (150) that I called, then checked the turn and called my 300. That line looks like a wide range: missed spade draws, A/K-type floats, small pairs (2/4), occasional 7s or river-improving Tens, and a few strong made hands (sets, A3 straights). I hold middle pair (5♦8♦) — not invincible but ahead of a lot of his missed-draw/float combos and some weak pairs. Pot is 1800 and he’s betting 700; calling 700 to win ~3200 requires ~22% equity. My hand has enough equity vs a wide river range to justify a call. Raising to 1400 would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and fold out worse hands or get me into tough spots vs actual value; folding would deny correct pot odds vs bluffs. I’ll call the 700 and go to showdown.

Preflop on the button with Ad6c offsuit. Excellent position against two opponents with deep stacks. A6o is a strong enough hand to raise for value and isolation, blocking strong aces like AA/AK. Pot is 150, standard raise sizing is 3x the BB to 300, which builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against the blinds. Opponent tendencies from history show Minni and Flash limp/call frequently but fold to raises often. Raising takes initiative and plays postflop from position if called. Folding or limping is too passive.

I call 250 to defend the small blind. A♠3♣ is marginal but the price is small relative to the pot and I want to see a flop rather than folding a lot of hands from the SB. I don't want to 3‑bet here: A3o has a weak kicker and a 3‑bet would frequently create a multiway spot or commit me out of position without clear fold equity. Postflop plan: proceed cautiously — if I miss I will give up to heavy continuation betting/pressure, if I hit an Ace I will control the pot size because my kicker is poor, and if the BB folds I can play heads‑up vs the button more aggressively when the board is favorable. If I face a sizable preflop 3‑bet I will fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Deuce of Clubs. Elon raised from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. While the price to call is only 200 chips to win a pot of 700 (giving me 3.5:1 pot odds), T2o is an extremely weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against two opponents. Given my healthy stack and the late stage of the tournament, there is no reason to defend with such a low-equity hand.

Flop (Kh 6d 9h) I completely missed with A♠3♣ — no pair, no heart, no backdoor except running spades is impossible. I'm out of position against the BTN raiser who opened wide from the CO/BTN; his range contains lots of Kx, broadways and many hands that will c-bet here. With a weak Ace-high and a deep stack advantage, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value play is pot control: check and see what Elon does. I’ll call small continuation bets to realize showdown value against bluffs and pairs that check back on later streets, but I’ll fold to large turns/river pressure or a heavy flop raise since my kicker and equity are poor. I won’t lead or check‑raise — that would be asking to play a bloated pot out of position with little equity. Plan: check now; if Elon bets small (≈≤1/2 pot) call and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to big flop raises or large turn sizing that close off my options.

Heads-up on the flop with Ad6c on Kh-6d-9h board. I have middle pair (6s) with Ace kicker, which is a strong hand here. The board is draw-heavy with a heart flush draw (Kh9h), open-ended straight draws (e.g., 7-8, 5-7, T-8), and overcard potential. Minni checked from the small blind after calling my button raise preflop, suggesting weakness or a draw. Betting extracts value from worse hands like weaker pairs, Ax high, or draws, while providing protection and building the pot. My position allows me to continue aggression if she raises. Half-pot sizing (around 350) is standard, but I'll size up slightly to 450 to charge draws more heavily and maintain fold equity against strong hands. Pot is 700, bet 450 commits appropriately given my 6700 stack and her deep 21900.

Flop (Kh 6d 9h): I completely missed with A♠3♣ and am out of position vs the BTN raiser. Elon’s c-bet of 450 (~39% of the pot) is small — exactly the sizing I planned to call and control the pot with. Calling 450 gives me ~28% pot odds (call 450 to win ~1600), and I’m getting the right price to see a turn against a wide BTN continuation range that contains lots of bluffs and weaker holdings. Raising would be over-aggressive with no pair and a poor kicker; folding is too passive given the odds and the chance to pick up value or see a free showdown. Plan: call the flop, keep the pot controlled, and fold to large turn aggression (or a raise/shove) unless I improve to an Ace on the turn, in which case I’ll reassess for value.

I missed the turn with A♠3♣ on Kh 6d 9h Kd. Elon lead‑bet the flop and I called to keep the pot controlled; his check on the turn looks like pot‑control with Kx or a medium hand rather than a sign to bluff shove. My Ace‑high has very poor showdown value here and no backdoor draws, so betting would be a thin bluff that folds out some worse hands but gets called or raised by many hands that beat me. I’ll keep to my original pot‑control plan: check and see the river, then fold to large aggression unless I pair up.

On the turn, the Kd pairs the top card, improving the board for sets and two-pair combos but also potentially improving draws or villain's Kx hands. I have middle pair with Ace kicker (6s Ac), solid showdown value that beats bluffs, 9x, weaker pairs, and missed draws from her checking range. Minni has checked twice after calling pre and flop, indicating capped range without top pair or better likely (would bet turn sometimes). Continuing aggression extracts value from her floaty calls seen in history (frequent calls with marginal hands/draws) and denies equity to any remaining draws (hearts, diamond flush possible, 78 straight). Position allows control. Pot 1600, bet ~60% pot (1000) sizes for value, charges draws, and commits reasonably from my 6250 stack while leaving room for river flexibility. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or losing value from worse.

Elon opened wide from the BTN, I called the small flop c-bet to keep the pot controlled with A♠3♣. The turn pairs the board (Kh 6d 9h Kd) and Elon bets 1,000 into 1,600 (pot now 2,600). Calling 1,000 to win ~3,600 requires ~28% equity, but my hand has essentially no showdown value (no pair, no relevant backdoors) — only ~6.5% to hit an Ace on the river. Elon’s line (open on BTN, small flop c-bet, firm turn bet) is exactly the line Kx and other value hands take; while he may bluff sometimes, his range is heavily weighted toward holdings that beat me. Raising out of position with a bare Ace is too risky and unlikely to fold enough better hands. I planned earlier to fold to large turn aggression unless I improved, and I didn’t. Fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

8♣4♣ is a very weak offsuit hand with almost no playability and is frequently dominated. Even though I’m on the button, both blinds are still to act so calling 100 or a small open to 200 will likely create a multiway pot with little fold equity and a high chance of facing a 3-bet or being outplayed postflop. With a healthy stack (21,450) and blinds small (50/100), there’s no reason to gamble with garbage — better to preserve chips and wait for a better stealing or isolated spot. Fold.

The button folded, leaving me in the small blind with Ace-Four offsuit. This is a strong hand to attack the big blind with in a heads-up situation. Elon (BB) has shown a mix of aggression and folding to 3-bets in previous hands. Raising to 300 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or see a flop as the aggressor.

Pocket kings (KsKc) is one of the best starting hands, crushing Flash's likely wide small blind opening range which often includes steals, suited connectors, Ax, and broadway cards. Minni has folded, so it's heads-up. Facing a 300 raise into 400 pot, I need to 3-bet for value, protection against set-mining hands or Ax that could flop an ace (about 12% chance vs my KK), and to build the pot OOP where position is a disadvantage. Standard sizing is 3x the open (900 total), which is aggressive but leaves room for 4-bets and commits us well with deep stacks (my 7750 vs Flash's 10400). Calling is too passive OOP and invites multiway pots or free flops for draws. Folding is insane.

Elon has been showing significant aggression in recent hands, and his 3-bet to 900 from the Big Blind suggests a strong range or a high-frequency bluffing strategy. Ace-Four offsuit is a marginal hand that performs poorly out of position in bloated, 3-bet pots. If I call, I will be playing a pot worth 1800 chips while out of position with a hand that is easily dominated or has little post-flop playability. Given the tournament stage and my current stack size, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips to a disadvantageous situation.

I am on the button with 8s 5c, which is a very weak hand. In a three-handed game, while positional advantage is important, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range. Given that the opponents in the blinds (Elon and Minni) have shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet recently, there is no reason to risk chips on a sub-optimal steal attempt. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

Pocket 5s is a strong hand in a 3-handed pot pre-flop from the small blind after Button folds. Standard play is to raise for value and to protect against overcards, while potentially isolating or stealing the big blind. Minni tends to call raises frequently from history, but 55 has excellent equity against her calling range. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is a balanced size that builds the pot without overcommitting early. With 81BB effective stack, this is low variance and maximizes EV over many hands.

Heads-up vs Elon (SB/button) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Tc Js — a strong, well-connected hand heads-up that plays very well against a wide SB opening range. Calling 200 is cheap (good pot odds), but I’m the chip leader (21350 vs 7850) and want to apply pressure/isolate the shorter-stacked opponent and build pots with a hand that has both straight/flush and two‑pair potential. I’ll 3-bet to a standard sizing (1000 total) — big enough to take down the pot preflop sometimes and to charge dominated/wide hands, but not so large that I overcommit. If Elon 4-bets big I’ll be willing to fold (JTo is not a 4-bet shove hand vs a strong 4-bet); if he calls I’ll proceed OOP carefully: c‑bet dry boards, control pot size on coordinated boards, and realize equity when appropriate.

Pocket 5s is a premium hand heads-up against Minni's BB 3-bet range 3-handed, which is wide based on her history of frequent raises and calls but folding to heavy aggression (e.g., multiple folds to 3-bets and 4-bets in prior hands). We have roughly 42-48% equity vs. a polarized range (QQ+, AK, suited broadways, Ax suited, some lower pairs/bluffs). Folding is too tight; calling 700 OOP deep-stacked risks difficult postflop spots with overcards, dominated draws, or reverse implied odds against her occasional monsters. 4-bet shoving our 78BB stack maximizes fold equity (~60-70% vs. her range) while extracting value from worse pairs (22-44), AK/AQ that can't call, and bluffs. Pot becomes ~13k post-shove; she needs ~38% equity to call profitably, which only premium hands (~6-8% of hands) have, but she folds most. This builds stack aggressively over remaining 28 hands.

I 3‑bett to 1,000 to isolate Elon and leverage my big stack, but his huge 4‑bet to 7,850 polarizes to premium value (AA/KK/QQ/AK) or a rare bluff. The pot is 8,850 and calling would cost me 6,850 more to play a ~15,700 pot — I’d need ~44% equity to break even, which JTs OOP unlikely has vs a polarized 4‑bet range. I also prefer preserving my 20k+ stack and waiting for better spots rather than getting committed out of position with a marginal hand. Fold and move on.

3h8s is a complete trash hand: offsuit, disconnected low cards with minimal playability post-flop. On the button with deep stacks (91BB), we have position, but this hand has no speculative value, straight potential, or domination potential. Opponents have large stacks and have shown aggression in history, but calling or raising would be spewing chips with such a weak range. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in this late tournament stage (hand 123/150). Stealing light is possible here with 3 players, but 38o is too weak even for a button steal given the gaps and low equity.

Elon folded so I'm heads-up against Flash in the big blind. KsTs suited is a strong, playable heads-up hand with good blocker value to broadway hands. Calling 50 is cheap but passive — I’d be giving up initiative and letting Flash see a flop for free. I want to take the lead, build the pot when I’m likely ahead, and fold out weaker holdings, so I’ll raise to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total). That gives me fold equity and a good pot size to play post-flop; if Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 4h. Elon folded on the button, and Minni raised to 300 from the Small Blind. The pot currently has 400 chips, and it costs me 200 more to call, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While 9-4 suited is a weak hand, it has reasonable playability in a heads-up pot where I will have position throughout the hand. Given the favorable odds and the fact that I am closing the action, I will call to see a flop.

Flop: Jh 9s Td. My KsTs = a pair of tens (second pair) with a strong K kicker and a backdoor spade possibility. Heads-up as the preflop raiser I have a range advantage and a lot of hands that benefit from a c-bet here (weaker tens, single-pair 9x, many overcards that will fold, and some draws). Villain’s calling range (BB) also contains dangerous hands that beat me — Jx, sets, two-pair combos, and the rare two-card straight (KQ, Q8) — so I don’t want to just check and give those free cards. I also don’t want to bloat the pot unnecessarily with a marginal holding, so a standard half-pot c-bet is best: it extracts value from worse hands, charges overcards/draws, and keeps the pot manageable if I face aggression. Plan: bet ~50% pot (300). If I get raised big, treat it as strong and fold; if called, re-evaluate on the turn (value-size up on improvement, slow/defend against aggression on scary cards).

I have middle pair (nines) on a very coordinated board of Jack-Ten-Nine. Minni has led out with a half-pot bet of 300 into 600. The pot odds are 3:1, which justifies a call with a pair and a backdoor flush draw, especially since I have position for the rest of the hand. However, the board is dangerous as any King, Queen, or Eight completes various straights, and my kicker (four) is very weak. I will call this flop bet to see if the turn improves my hand or if Minni slows down, but I must proceed with extreme caution on future streets if the action continues.

Check. I have second pair (Tens) with a strong K kicker after leading the hand preflop and c-betting the flop. Pot is 1,200 and the turn 8♥ is a dangerous card — it completes many Qx and 7x straights and helps combos that already beat me (Jx, T9, sets). Betting here would bloat the pot and get called or raised by many hands that dominate me, while only getting thin value from worse hands. With stacks deep relative to the pot, the prudent line is pot control: check and see what Flash does. Plan: if he checks back I go to the river and re-evaluate; if he fires a small probe (≈≤600, ~half-pot) I’ll call to realize equity and see the river; if he bets big or raises I’ll fold — avoid committing with a vulnerable one-pair hand.

On a board of Jh-9s-Td-8h, there is a four-card straight present (8-9-T-J). I have a pair of nines and have picked up a heart flush draw on the turn. Minni has checked. While I have a pair, it is very weak on this coordinated board where any Queen or Seven makes a straight. However, my flush draw gives me significant equity. Checking behind is the best play here; it allows me to realize my equity for free and prevents me from getting check-raised by a straight or a higher set, while also keeping the pot manageable for my marginal made hand.

Board: Jh 9s Td 8h 2h. I hold KsTs — second pair (tens) with a very strong K kicker. After I c-bet the flop and both of us checked the turn the action line points to a wide, medium-strength calling range from Flash (Tx with worse kickers, 9x, some Jx, mid pairs, and scattered connectors). The river 2h brings a third heart but only completes a flush for opponents holding two hearts (a small portion of his range). My hand has good showdown value and can get called by worse tens, 9x, and some floating broadways, so I want to extract thin value without bloating the pot or turning my hand into a bluff catcher. I’ll make a small value bet (~1/3 pot) to charge worse hands and preserve fold equity; if I face a large raise I’ll give it up (likely behind to two-pair/sets/flush), if he just calls we see showdown.

I have a 9-high flush on a board of Jh-9s-Td-8h-2h. The turn 8h completed potential straights (any Q or 7), and the river 2h brought the third heart. Minni led with a small 1/3 pot bet of 400 into 1200. While a flush is a very strong hand, the board is highly coordinated and Minni's range as the pre-flop raiser from the small blind includes several higher hearts (Ah, Kh, Qh, or 7h). Calling is the most prudent action here; I beat all straights, sets, and two-pairs, but I am vulnerable to a higher flush, making a raise potentially value-owning myself.

Three-handed on the button with J♣9♦ (offsuit) is worth taking the initiative. With deep stacks (19,450) and only the SB and BB to beat, I want to raise to isolate and use position postflop rather than limp and surrender initiative. A standard 3x BB open to 300 chips keeps the pot manageable with a marginal hand while putting pressure on the blinds and giving me favorable fold equity. If I get called I'll c-bet favorable flops and fold to heavy resistance; fold to a large 3-bet out of position.

I hold Ace-King of hearts, which is a premium starting hand. Minni has opened from the button, a position where players typically raise with a wide range of hands. To capitalize on the strength of my hand, gain the initiative, and potentially force the Big Blind to fold, I will 3-bet. A raise to 1000 is consistent with my previous 3-bet sizing in this tournament and puts pressure on the button.

Preflop in the big blind with Kd9h offsuit facing a button raise to 300 (3BB) and small blind 3-bet to 1000 (10BB). My hand is marginal at best - king-high with a weak kicker, offsuit, and poor playability postflop. Flash's 3-bet from the small blind over a button open suggests a strong range (JJ+, AKo/s, maybe AQs), and Minni can still 4-bet or call with strength. Pot odds are about 1.55:1 (900 to call into 1400), but my equity is likely under 30% against villain ranges, and going multiway deep-stacked with this hand risks my stack unnecessarily. With 90BB effective stack and late in tournament (124/150), folding preserves stack for better spots. No fold equity worth a shove or 4-bet.

Flash 3-bet to 1,000 after my 300 button open. Jc9d is too weak for a light 4‑bet but has decent playability and equity in position. The pot is 1,400 and I must put in 700 more to call — that gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). Flash has shown a lot of preflop aggression and his 3‑bet range will include bluffs, so calling keeps weaker hands in and lets me play the flop last. Plan: call, evaluate the flop — proceed aggressively if I hit or have good turns/river equity, fold to heavy action on dry boards. Avoid bloating the pot with a marginal 4‑bet.

On a Jack-high flop with two spades, I have two overcards and a backdoor heart draw. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I maintain the range advantage and initiative. A continuation bet here can represent a strong overpair or a Jack, putting pressure on Minni's middle pairs or missed hands. Betting around 40% of the pot (800) is a balanced size that keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to potentially take it down immediately or evaluate the turn.

Board: Jh 4s 5s. I hold Jc9d — top pair with a medium kicker in position. Preflop I opened and Flash 3‑bet to 1000; that range contains premium value (QQ+, AK, strong broadways) but also a fair number of bluffs and suited connectors. His flop lead of 800 into a 2900 pot is small (≈28% pot) — a probing c‑bet that can be wide and include many draws and weaker jacks. 

Pot odds: calling 800 into 2900 means I’d be risking 800 to win ~3700 (≈21.6% equity needed). Top pair here clears that threshold versus a typical 3‑bet/continuation range, and being in position I can control the pot size and extract value or fold later. 

Raising now would bloat the pot with a marginal kicker and run the risk of committing to big action from hands that currently dominate me (overpairs, stronger Jx). The small bet size makes calling the straightforward +EV line: keep worse hands and draws in, see a turn, and re‑evaluate. Plan: call 800, continue on turns that improve me or where Flash shows weakness; proceed cautiously if a spade or heavy action appears.

The turn 2d is largely a blank. Since Minni called the flop bet, she likely has a piece of the board (a Jack or a pocket pair) or a flush draw. Continuing to bet here with Ace-high out of position is risky, as it's unlikely to fold out a Jack and leaves me vulnerable to a raise. By checking, I can control the size of the pot, potentially see a free river card to hit my overcards, or evaluate her bet sizing if she chooses to lead out.

Flash checked the turn after a small flop lead — that check is consistent with weakness/pot control or a disguised strong hand, but his preflop 3‑bet + small flop bet range will include many weaker Jx, pocket pairs, floats and some two‑spade combo draws. I have top pair (J♣) with a mediocre kicker (9♦) in position. Checking back surrenders value and lets draws see a cheap river; betting protects vs backdoor equity and extracts value from worse Jx, 4s/5s/2s floats and small pairs. I’ll make a modest value/protection bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) so I get called by worse while keeping the pot controllable. Pot = 3700, so bet 1500. If he check‑raises big I’ll assume a very strong hand (overpair/set) and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river for further value or pot control.

I am holding Ace-King high on a Jack-high board. While AK is a premium pre-flop hand, its value has diminished significantly after being called on the flop and facing a bet on the turn. Minni's 1,500 bet into the 3,700 pot (making it 5,200 total) represents a reasonably strong range, likely a Jack, a pocket pair, or a strong draw. My direct equity based on outs (3 Aces and 3 Kings) is roughly 13%, which does not satisfy the pot odds of approximately 22%. Given Minni's demonstrated aggression in previous hands and the risk of facing a much larger bet on the river if I don't improve, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my 81BB stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 3d 2d, which is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. While the button typically opens a wide range, 32s is at the very bottom of that range. Given that Minni (the big blind) has been playing very aggressively and holds a significant chip lead, and Elon (small blind) is also capable of playing back, there is no need to commit chips with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity, as I still have 96 big blinds and plenty of time left in the tournament.

Preflop in SB with 4c8s offsuit, a complete trash hand with poor playability, low equity against any reasonable BB defending range. Minni has been aggressive historically but has a massive stack (212BB), making her less likely to fold BB. Folding the SB is standard to preserve chips for better spots in this tournament with 25 hands left. No steal value here as the hand can't realize equity postflop well.

Preflop on the button with JJ, a premium pair in a short-handed pot facing only blinds. No raises before me, pot is 150 with 100 to call. Standard play is to raise for value and position, isolating against likely weak blind ranges. Minni and Flash have shown aggression in history but JJ crushes their calling ranges. Raise to 3x the BB (300 total) to build the pot while keeping it manageable. Avoid flat-calling to protect against multiway pots or dominated draws later.

I’m in the small blind with 4♦4♣ (small pair) facing a BTN open to 300 and the BB yet to act. With deep effective stacks this is a classic set‑mining spot: calling preserves stack, gives good implied odds to hit a set, and avoids bloating the pot out of position. A tiny 3‑bet to 500 is too small to fold out hands and a larger 3‑bet invites a squeeze from the BB — I don’t want to build a big OOP pot with a vulnerable small pair. I’ll call the 250 more (300 total committed) and re‑evaluate on the flop: proceed to extract value if I hit a set, fold or play cautiously if I miss and face heavy action, and fold to a large 3‑bet preflop unless the price/reads justify continuing.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Trey offsuit. Elon raised from the Button to 300, and Minni called from the Small Blind. I need to put in 200 more to call into a total pot of 700. This gives me pot odds of 4.5 to 1, which is extremely favorable for a hand containing an Ace. While Ace-Trey is not a premium hand, it has decent equity in a three-way pot, and closing the action allows me to see the flop cheaply without further raises. Given the stack sizes and the tournament stage, calling is the most efficient play to protect my stack while maintaining potential to hit a strong board.

I flopped a set (4♦4♣ on 4♥6♣3♠). This is a very strong but vulnerable-to-rare cooler hand and I'm first to act out of position against two opponents (BTN Elon and BB Flash). The flop is dry and low so lots of their calling ranges (overcards, medium pairs, some pocket pairs) will continue — I want to extract value and deny a free turn card. A small-to-medium bet will keep worse hands and floats in, while not folding them out with a large polarizing size. I'll bet roughly 1/3 pot to induce calls and start building the pot; if I face a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (big raises usually represent rare very strong hands).

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a low board (4-6-3). Minni's lead of 300 into the 900 pot is a small sizing (1/3 pot), giving me favorable 4:1 pot odds. While bottom pair is a weak holding, it has significant showdown value in a three-way pot and the potential to improve to two pair or trips. Folding here would be too tight given the price. I will call to see the turn and gauge the original raiser's (Elon) reaction.

JJ is a strong overpair on a dry, low rainbow flop (4h-6c-3s) with minimal straight draws (gutshots like 2-5 or 5-7). Minni's small donk bet from SB after calling my preflop raise often represents weakness or a draw trying to take down the pot cheaply; Flash's flat call doesn't change that much. Pot is 1500, bet is only 300 (~20% pot), so raising builds the pot for value while protecting against draws/implied odds. Raising to 4x (1200 total) is standard: charges draws, gets value from worse pairs/floats, and my position allows control. Stacks are deep enough (me 8700, Flash 9000), but history shows opponents fold to aggression often. Calling keeps pot small but invites multiway trouble on turn; folding is too weak.

I flopped a set on a very dry 4♥-6♣-3♠ board. I bet small to induce calls, Flash did call, and now Elon (BTN) has raised to 1,200 — a sizing that can represent a set (66/33), a single pair with a big kicker (A6/A3), or an over-aggressive bluff. My hand is near-nut and I want to extract value while denying free cards, but I also want to keep worse one-pair hands and floats in the pot. I’ll make the minimum reraise to 2,100 to build the pot and charge Ax/6x/3x and other callers; if I face a large 4‑bet or shove I’ll re-evaluate (those lines usually indicate the rare hands that beat me).

The flop action is extremely heavy. Minni led into the pot, was raised by Elon, and then responded with a 3-bet to 2100. This sequence of actions on a low, connected board (4-6-3) typically signifies monster hands such as sets (66, 44, 33), two pair (64s), or even a straight (57s). While I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker, I am almost certainly crushed by both opponents' ranges. Continuing in a 3-bet pot with bottom pair while sandwiched between two aggressive players is a losing play. Folding allows me to preserve my 9000-chip stack for more favorable spots.

JJ remains an overpair on a dry 4h-6c-3s flop with limited draws (gutshots like 2-5 or 5-7, no flush draws). Minni's line—loose preflop call from SB, small donk bet (20% pot), then 3-bet after my raise—polarizes her range to value (sets like 33/44/66, two-pair 3-4/3-6/4-6 unlikely) or bluffs/draws (A5s, 57, air trying to rep strength). Flash's fold doesn't add much. Pot odds excellent: 900 to call into 4500 (16.7% equity needed, JJ likely 70%+ vs her range). Deep stacks (mine 7500 post-call ~6600), position allows seeing turn cheaply; can shove blanks or fold to scary cards/brushes. Folding too tight short-handed; raising to 3000 commits ~40% stack, risks vs sets while she can fold bluffs. Call balances range, realizes equity, exploits her history of folding to continued aggression later.

The turn 9 changes the board texture and my decision. I currently have trips (444) but the 9 gives my opponent a very plausible hand that now beats me — most importantly pocket 99 (an overpair on the flop that would reasonably raise to 1,200 and then call my 2,100). Elon’s flop line (raise then call my reraise) can also include 66/33 (also beating me) and strong A‑x hands that I still crush. With Elon the shorter stack (≈6.6k) any sizable bet here can induce an all‑in shove; while I can call, I don’t want to bloat the pot further with a hand that is not the absolute nuts and that is vulnerable to a non‑trivial subset of his range.

I prefer pot control: check the turn to keep worse one‑pair/overpair hands and floats in the pot, to avoid getting shoved on by a hand that currently dominates me (99/66/33), and to induce bluffs or thin value bets I can then extract from. If Elon bets small I’ll call to realize equity and extract value; if he shoves I’ll evaluate the sizing/read but calling is certainly on the table given my deep stack advantage. Checking preserves options and maximizes my long‑term EV in this spot.

JJ remains a strong overpair on this dry turn board (4h6c3s9d), rainbow with limited draw completion (9d is somewhat blank, no new straights or flushes, though it fills gutshots like 57 or adds to 789). Minni's line continues to be polarized: her preflop call from SB, small flop donk-bet (likely weak/draw), aggressive 3-bet vs my raise (either strong made hands like sets 33/44/66 ~6 combos or two-pair combos like 43/46 low, or bluffs with missed draws like A5, 25, 57, air). Flash folded, so heads-up. Her turn check after 3-betting flop suggests either pot control with a monster (hoping to trap) or weakness/giving up on a bluff/draw that missed. With position, pot 5400 (~82% of my 6600 stack), and deep effective (my stack shallower), checking realizes equity vs her value range cheaply and induces bluffs on river, but risks giving free cards to stubborn draws and missing value from worse hands (overcards like AQo, KQo pairs, Tx). Betting extracts value from her weaker value range (Ax pairs, 77-88-TT), protects vs remaining draws (2 outs for 25 straight), and denies equity. Half-pot bet of 2700 builds pot to ~11k, leaving me ~3900 behind (good fold equity if she bluffs), charges draws, and balances my range (I've shown strength with flop raise). History shows Minni calls stations but folds to aggression (e.g., Hand 51, 111); short-handed late tournament, maximize by betting thin value here. Shove too polarized/overplays; small bet too checky. Bet 2700 optimal.

I have trips (4♣4♦ with 4♥ on board) on a 4♥-6♣-3♠-9♦ board. Elon (BTN) opened preflop, then took an aggressive flop line (raise to 1,200 and called my 2,100 reraise) which can include sets (66/33), strong overpairs (99/TT+), and some strong A-x/6x/3x combos. The turn 9 gives him a clear “beat me” candidate (99) and makes my hand vulnerable to a non-trivial subset of his range, but folding trips here is too weak. Raising to 5,400 would effectively force Elon all‑in and mainly get called by the very hands that currently beat me (sets/overpairs), so a raise is low EV and needlessly bloats the pot. Calling 2,700 keeps worse one‑pair hands and thin value/bluff combos in the pot, preserves pot control, and allows Elon to commit his short stack if he wants — I can then decide on an all‑in jam. I’ll call 2,700 to extract value while avoiding committing more when I could be behind to a small but important portion of his range.

I have trips (4♣4♦ with 4♥ on board) but the river Q is a dangerous card — it gives Elon plausible hands that now beat me (Qx, and the sets/overpairs I worried about such as 99/66/33). Flash folded on the flop so this is heads‑up and Elon is short (≈3.9k) which makes any river shove realistic. Betting here will mainly fold out the worse one‑pair hands I want value from and will get called/raised by the very thin portion of his range that currently beats me — raising/leading is low EV. I prefer pot control: check to induce a bluff or a thin value bet and keep worse hands in; if Elon fires a small/medium bet I’ll call for value, and if he shoves his 3.9k I’ll call as well given the pot odds (3.9k into ~10.8k pot → need ≈26–27% equity) and the reasonable frequency of bluffs/semi‑bluffs in his range. Therefore I check and evaluate his river action.

JJ remains a strong overpair on this river board (4h6c3s9dQh), beating any non-Q pair, most Qx hands (blocks QQ), and all bluffs/draws that missed. No flush completed (only two hearts), no straight (possible 5-7 missed 9d/turn, but unlikely given her line). Minni's full line—loose preflop SB call, small flop donk (weak/protection), huge 3bet (polarized to sets 33/44/66 ~6 combos, rare two-pair 43/46/34s, or bluffs like A5/27/57/A-high), turn call of 2700 (pot-committing her somewhat if bluffing), and river check (pot control with monster or giving up)—puts her range weighted toward value but with bluff catching equity for us. Pot 10800 vs my 3900 short stack (36% pot shove), position, and history of Minni folding to aggression (e.g., Hands 51,111,120) while calling stations sometimes. Shoving maximizes EV: extracts from worse (Qx, TT+, Ax pairs ~20-30% her range), massive fold equity vs bluffs (~40% equity needed but JJ crushes), denies river calls to missed draws. Checking realizes equity but misses value and risks chops; smaller bet risks call-only-from-better. Late tournament (126/150, 3 players), ICM pressure low with short stack—jam thin value to chip up or bust fighting.

I have trips (4♦4♣ with 4♥ on board). I checked the river to keep the pot controlled because the Q is a dangerous overcard that enables some of his value hands. Elon (BTN) has shoved his remaining stack (3,900) and is now all‑in; Flash folded earlier so it’s heads‑up. The pot shown is 14,700; calling 3,900 would make the final pot ~18,600, so I need roughly 3,900/18,600 ≈ 21% equity to break even. Elon’s line (open BTN, big flop raise, call my reraise, turn bet, shove river) is value‑heavy and includes sets that beat me (66, 33, 99 on the turn) and pocket QQ that trips on the river, but those combos are relatively limited (only a dozen or so pure crushers). He also can have many one‑pair/A‑x hands or thin value hands and occasional bluffs given his short stack. Raising isn’t an option (he’s all‑in) and folding here gives up too much expected value given the pot odds. I’ll call the 3,900 to realize equity and extract value from worse hands.

Heads-up preflop on the button (SB) with K♦J♠ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. I want initiative and fold equity against the big blind, and this hand plays well postflop deep-stacked. Standard exploitative sizing here is to open to ~3x the BB; raising to 300 (total) pressures wide defending ranges Flash has shown and lets me dictate the action. Plan: if he 3-bets small I can call and play postflop; fold to a large 4-bet/shove depending on sizing and pot odds.

I am holding 8-3 offsuit in the big blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker and performs very poorly out of position, especially against an opponent with a significantly larger stack. Although I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, the lack of playability and low equity realization of this hand makes it a clear fold. I will wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

Holding 10-8 of hearts on the button, this is a strong starting hand for heads-up or short-handed play. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to take the initiative and see how Minni responds from the big blind. This hand has good playability post-flop if called, and I want to maintain an aggressive profile given my shorter stack compared to the chip leader.

Heads-up with the button (Flash) opening to 300 from the SB. My Js5h is marginal/off-suit, but in heads-up the SB open is wide and I should defend the BB frequently. Calling costs me 200 more (total committed = 300 including my BB) to win a ~600 pot (good pot odds) and preserves flexibility post‑flop. 3‑betting out of position with J5o is a losing line long‑term and folding is too tight given the price and deep stacks. I’ll call, see a flop, and proceed cautiously — fold to strong action on dry boards and realize equity when I hit a pair or backdoor draws.

Check. Heads‑up vs the SB who opened very wide preflop; my J♠5♥ missed this low, two‑heart flop (2♣ 4♥ 6♥). I only have a 4‑out gutshot to a 3 and a long backdoor heart route—roughly ~16% to hit by the river—so no reason to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand. With deep stacks and a small pot (600) I’ll keep the pot controllable: check and give Flash the chance to c‑bet. I’ll call small continuation bets to realize equity but fold to large/overly aggressive sizing since my showdown value is weak. If he checks behind I get a free turn card to improve and can barreled later when I hit.

On a flop of 2c-4h-6h, I have a flush draw with my 8h-Th. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of 350 to apply pressure and build the pot for my equity. This board is relatively safe for my range, and I want to realize my draw's potential or take down the pot immediately.

I checked the flop and Flash c‑bet 350 into a 600 pot (pot now 950). With J♠5♥ on 2♣–4♥–6♥ I have no pair, a gutshot to a 3 (~16–17% to hit by the river) and a remote double‑heart backdoor (~4%). Calling 350 would cost me 350 to win a 1,300 pot (after my call) so I need ~27% equity to break even vs a pure call. My raw draw equity (~20%) is a bit below that, but in heads‑up play Flash opens very wide and c‑bets frequently — I’ll often win at showdown if he’s barreling air or check the turn — so calling is a reasonable, low‑risk way to realize equity and keep the pot controllable. A raise here is an unnecessary bloating/bluff that could induce a shove I don’t want to face OOP with J5o. Plan: call the 350, re‑evaluate on the turn and fold to large, committed aggression unless I improve (pair, 3 for the straight, or pickup a strong heart).

Check. After calling the flop with J♠5♥ I missed the Queen turn entirely — I have no pair, no meaningful backdoor outs and essentially zero raw equity now. Flash opened very wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, so some portion of his range is air or small pairs, but betting out OOP with this hand is unnecessary risk: a bluff here can be check‑raised or called by a lot of hands that beat me (Qx, any pair, Ax, broadways). Pot control is the priority — the pot is already 1,300 and I don’t want to bloat it with a worthless hand. I’ll check and take a free river to try to catch a pair; if Flash fires a substantial bet on the turn I’ll fold, and I’ll only call small, non‑committing bets that the sizing makes correct to realize showdown equity.

On a board of 2c-4h-6h-Qd, I have an 8-high flush draw with my 8h-Th. The Queen on the turn is an overcard that theoretically favors my pre-flop raising range, but Minni has shown in previous hands that she is quite 'sticky' and willing to call down with middle or bottom pairs. Given my stack size relative to hers and the fact that she has checked to me, I will check behind to take a free card and attempt to realize my equity. This keeps the pot manageable and allows me to potentially hit my flush or a pair on the river without risking a check-raise on the turn.

I have J♠5♥ and missed the entire board (2♣ 4♥ 6♥ Q♦ K♦). I called the flop to realize my weak backdoor equity and checked the turn for pot control after Flash checked back. On the river I have no pair and essentially zero showdown value. There’s no value-bet I can make, and a bluff is thin: the Q/K on the turn/river give many hands legitimate value and Flash opened very wide preflop but also c-bet the flop, then checked the turn — showing some weakness but not enough to risk a sizable bluff OOP. Best line is to keep the pot small and avoid bloating with air: check and fold to any meaningful river bet, only calling an extremely small, cheap bluff. Preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot.

On a board of 2c-4h-6h-Qd-Kd, I have missed my flush draw and currently hold only Ten-high. Showdown value is non-existent. However, the board runout of a Queen on the turn and a King on the river is significantly better for my perceived pre-flop raising range than for Minni's defending range. Minni has checked the river after calling a flop bet and seeing the turn checked through. This suggests she likely has a weak made hand, such as a small pair (6x, 4x) or a missed straight draw that she's given up on. While Minni has shown some 'stickiness' in previous hands, the presence of two overcards (Q and K) makes it very difficult for her to call a substantial river bet with a weak pair. I will bet 850 (approximately 65% of the pot) to represent a hand like AK, KQ, or even a slow-played set, forcing her to fold her marginal bluff-catchers.

I have J♠5♥ on 2♣–4♥–6♥–Q♦–K♦: no pair, no meaningful blockers and essentially zero showdown equity. Pot is 2,150 and Flash bet 850; calling 850 to win ~3,000 requires ~28% equity, which I don’t have except vs pure river bluffs. Flash’s line (wide open preflop, flop c‑bet, checked turn, medium river stab) can contain thin value (Qx/Kx/paired low cards) as well as some bluffs, but the bet size is big enough that folding is the +EV choice. Raising as a bluff OOP with this hand is too risky (he still has 7,400 behind and can call or shove). I’ll preserve my deep stack and fold.

Heads-up on the button (SB) I can open very wide. J♠2♥ is weak, but raising is the default to seize initiative, deny the BB cheap flops, and use my positional advantage. I’ll make a small, standard preflop raise to 2BB (200 total) to keep the pot manageable; plan is to fold to a 3-bet with this holding and play postflop aggressively in position if called (c-bet dry boards, control pot size on coordinated boards).

Q9o is a very standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a button min-raise. With pot odds of 4:1, I only need about 20% equity to make this call profitable, and Q9o performs well against a typical button opening range. I will call to see a flop and play the hand from there.

On this flop (Ah-Jc-Td), I have an open-ended straight draw with Q9s. While this board likely hits Minni's button-raising range very hard (containing many Aces, Jacks, and Tens), my draw is strong enough to continue. Playing out of position, I will start by checking to the pre-flop aggressor to see how she interacts with this texture. If she bets, I have the direct odds to call most reasonable sizes given my 8 outs to a straight.

Flop: A♥ J♣ T♦ — I have top pair with a very weak kicker (J♠2♥). I opened preflop so Flash’s calling range is wide (lots of Ax, broadways, pairs and some connectors), and his check here includes many hands I currently beat (weaker jacks, some tens, KJ/QJ, medium pairs) as well as some hands that beat me (Ax, KQ). This is a good spot to c-bet for value and protection: a half‑pot bet extracts from worse pairs and Jx hands, charges broadway/straight draws, and keeps the pot controllable given my poor kicker and deep stacks. Plan: bet ~50% pot (200). If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (second barrel on a blank, give up or pot‑control vs heavy action or an Ace/King/Queen); if he check‑raises big I’m folding—my kicker makes me poorly positioned vs strong lines.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a very coordinated board (Ah-Jc-Td) with my Qd9s. Minni's 200-chip bet into a 400-chip pot gives me 3:1 odds to call. With 8 outs to a straight (four Kings and four Eights), my equity is approximately 32% by the river, which makes calling mathematically profitable given the current pot odds. I will call to see if I can improve on the turn while keeping the pot manageable against what is likely a strong range from the button on this texture.

On the turn, the board has paired with the Ten of Spades. While this card doesn't directly improve my hand, I still hold an open-ended straight draw (any King or Eight). Given the board is highly coordinated (A-J-T-T) and hits a button-opening range well, I will check to the aggressor to see how she plays this street. If she checks behind, I get a free card; if she bets, I will evaluate based on the price offered.

I have top pair (J♠2♥) but a very weak kicker. Preflop I opened and Flash called; he then check‑called my ~200 c‑bet on A♥ J♣ T♦, which is a wide range (Ax, Jx, Tx, KQ, medium pairs, some draws). The turn T♠ pairs the board, which helps Tens (converts many flop calls into trips) and still leaves lots of hands that beat me (Ax, any Ten, KQ straights, better Jx with superior kicker). Flash checked the turn — likely pot control or weakness rather than a clear value shove — so I should avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. I will check to control pot size, keep bluffs in his range, and see how he reacts. Plan if he bets: call small‑to‑medium bets (~≤50% pot, ~≤400) to realize equity vs bluffs/worse Jx, but fold to large overbets or jams (>~pot) where I am often crushed. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river and either take a small thin‑value stab on safe runouts or check to showdown.

On a board of Ah-Jc-Td-Ts-4d, I have completely missed my open-ended straight draw and am left with Queen-high. My opponent, Minni, raised from the button and bet the flop, which is a board that hits a button-opening range very hard. Her check on the turn suggests she might have a medium-strength hand like a Jack or a small pocket pair, or perhaps she is pot-controlling with a weak Ace. Queen-high has almost no showdown value, but bluffing here is risky because Minni's range contains many hands that will not fold to a single river bet (Aces and Jacks). Given the significant chip lead she holds, she is likely to look me up with any pair. Therefore, I will check and prepared to fold if she bets, as I cannot profitably represent a hand strong enough to make her fold better.

Board: A♥ J♣ T♦ T♠ 4♦. I hold J♠2♥ — top pair with a very weak kicker. Flash called my flop c‑bet but then checked both the turn (when the board paired tens) and the river. That line looks like pot‑control/weakness or a slow‑played strong hand, but there’s a lot of Ax and Tens in his calling range that beat me outright. A small thin‑value bet on the river would occasionally extract from worse Jx, but it will also get called by many hands that beat me (A‑x, any Ten) and won’t fold enough better hands to make it +EV. Given my poor kicker and the paired turn increasing the number of hands that dominate me, the prudent line is to avoid bloating the pot and check to showdown. If he had bet the river I’d consider calling small‑to‑medium bets (~≤50% pot ≈400) to catch bluffs/worse Js but would fold to large overbets or jams.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 5d. This is a very weak starting hand. While it's tempting to complete the small blind for only 50 chips more or attempt a steal, my opponent Minni has a significant chip lead and has shown a willingness to defend the Big Blind aggressively. With only 20 hands remaining in the tournament and my stack being considerably smaller than Minni's, I prefer to fold these bottom-tier hands and wait for a more advantageous spot to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Q♦4♦ — a weak-to-middling but suited hand that plays well in position. I have the chip lead (30.8k vs 9k) and the BB will defend wide, so I want to seize initiative and avoid giving them a free look. Folding is too nitty and calling 50 is passive (gives the BB good odds). I’ll open-raise to a standard 3 BB size (300 total) to build the pot, apply pressure, and take advantage of position. Size is big enough to gain fold equity but not so big as to overcommit if I face a 3-bet; I’ll re-evaluate on the flop based on action.

I am holding K2o in the Big Blind. Minni raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given the 2:1 pot odds and the nature of heads-up play, K2o is strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a standard open. I will call to see a flop and look for a King or favorable board texture, while remaining cautious of Minni's large stack advantage.

I am the Big Blind and it is my turn to act first on the flop. The board is Ts 5h 5d, which is a paired board. I hold King-high (Ks 2d). While this board is relatively dry, I do not have a piece of it yet. In heads-up play, King-high has some showdown value, but it is not a hand I want to lead out with into the pre-flop aggressor. I will check to see how Minni acts and evaluate her bet sizing if she decides to continuation-bet.

Heads-up on a very dry flop T♠‑5♥‑5♦. I raised preflop and have position with Q♦4♦ — I missed but hold a tiny backdoor diamond chance and the initiative. Flash in the BB defended wide and checked, so a continuation bet will fold a lot of unpaired overcards and small hands while keeping the pot controlled. I’ll size small (≈50% pot) to maximize fold equity without overcommitting; if I get a sizable raise I can fold this marginal hand, if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (look to barrel only if the turn improves my equity or gives a favorable runout).

On a dry, paired board of Ts 5h 5d, I am holding King-high. While I missed the flop, King-high has significant showdown value in heads-up play and is often ahead of many of Minni's continuation-betting 'air' hands. Minni is betting 300 into a 600 pot, giving me 3:1 odds. Folding King-high here would be too exploitable. I will call to see how the turn develops and evaluate if Minni continues her aggression.

The turn card is the Jack of Spades, which doesn't improve my King-high but adds potential straight and flush draws to the board. As the out-of-position player, checking is the standard and most prudent play with a marginal hand like King-high. I will check to see how Minni acts on this turn card.

I’m the preflop aggressor and c‑betted the dry T♠‑5♥‑5♦ flop; I hold Q♦4♦ and did not pair. The turn J♠ does nothing for my hand and, crucially, kills my backdoor diamond plan (I needed both turn+river diamonds to make a flush). Flash defended wide preflop and called my flop bet, so his calling range includes many hands that will continue to a turn barrel (T, Jx, small/medium pairs, stubborn overcards). A thin turn bluff here has poor equity and runs the risk of getting called by too many hands — I don’t want to bloat the pot with a garbage hand. With a big chip lead (30.3k vs 8.5k) I should pot‑control and keep my bluffs for a cleaner river spot. I’ll check back the turn, preserve my stack, and re‑evaluate on the river: fire a bluff only if the river and opponent’s line make it credible, otherwise fold to real aggression.

The board has paired to four-of-a-kind Fives. My best five-card hand is now four Fives with a King kicker (5-5-5-5-K). This is the second-best possible hand, losing only to an Ace. As the first to act on the river, checking is the most strategic move. If I bet, Minni is likely to fold any hand that doesn't contain an Ace or a King, meaning I gain no extra value from her 'air'. By checking, I induce Minni to potentially bluff with a weaker hand or a missed draw, and I can comfortably call any standard-sized bet she makes. If she also checks, we go to a showdown where I am very likely to win or at least chop the pot.

Board: T♠ 5♥ 5♦ J♠ 5♣ — the river bricks a 5 and leaves trips on board. Any opponent holding a pocket pair (22–AA) or a T or J now has a full house; quads are possible only if they hold the lone remaining 5. Flash defended wide preflop and called my small flop c‑bet, so his flop‑calling range likely contains many pocket pairs and T's that have just turned into full houses. I hold Q♦4♦ — no pair, no draw, zero showdown value. A river bluff here has very poor fold equity and a high cost when called or raised. With a big chip lead (30.3k vs ~8.5k) there's no reason to bloat the pot in a thin, low‑credibility bluff spot. I’ll check and preserve my stack; if Flash bets I’ll fold to avoid risking a lot to a likely full house.

I am on the button with Qc2c. In a heads-up situation, any suited Queen is well within the standard opening range. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or play the pot with positional advantage if called.

Facing a button open to 300 from SB with me in the BB holding 3♠6♥. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (300 total). Heads-up opens are wide, and calling here is cheap — calling 200 into 400 gives ~25% break-even equity. 3‑6o is weak but has enough playability heads‑up to see a flop and fold or proceed based on texture. I don’t want to 3‑bet/bluff with such a trashy hand (no meaningful blockers and limited postflop equity), and folding surrenders too much given the pot odds and opponent’s likely wide range. I’ll defend and see a flop, then play cautiously.

I have bottom pair (3♠) with a weak 6 kicker on Qh‑3h‑7s. Flash opened preflop from the button and will c‑bet a wide range, so my hand is marginal and vulnerable to any Qx, 7x, overpairs or heart draws. Leading out here gains little — thin value vs worse and poor blockers for a credible bluff — so I’ll check and control the pot, call small continuation bets for the price, fold to a large shove or sizeable overbet, and re‑evaluate on the turn.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a relatively dry board, though there is a heart flush draw. In heads-up play, top pair is a very strong hand. Minni has checked to me, and I should make a continuation bet to get value from weaker hands like a 7, a 3, or various draws, and to protect against overcards or completed draws on later streets. A bet of 300 into a 600 pot (50% pot) is a standard size to accomplish these goals.

I have bottom pair (3♠) with a weak 6 kicker on Qh-3h-7s. Flash’s flop bet is small relative to the pot (300 into the current pot), so calling 300 gives me ~25% break-even equity — cheap enough to continue heads‑up against what is likely a very wide c‑betting range. Folding would be too nitty here; raising is unnecessary and risky with a vulnerable hand (I have no useful blockers and I’m behind most Qx/7x hands and ahead only of pure bluffs). I’ll call to control the pot, see the turn, and re-evaluate: fold to a large raise or shove if pressured, but continue vs small turn bets or check‑calls on safer turn cards.

I have bottom pair (3♠) with a weak 6 kicker on Qh‑3h‑7s‑5d. Flash was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop, but then checked the turn — that line suggests a wide range with many marginal hands and some bluffs, but also lots of hands that comfortably beat me (Qx, 7x, 5x). Pot is 1,200 and I have no compelling blockers or fold‑equity to make a profitable bluff; a bet here would either get called by better hands or force me into a tough decision versus a raise. Given stacks and heads‑up dynamics, pot control is the best line: check back to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable. Plan for the river: call small, non‑threatening bets but fold to large overbets or shoves; if Flash checks back the river I’ll re‑evaluate for a small value bet or showdown.

I have top pair (Queens) with a weak kicker. The turn 5d is a blank card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Since Minni checked again, I will place a value bet to charge any heart flush draws and to get value from weaker hands like a 7 or a 3. A half-pot bet of 600 is sufficient to keep the pot manageable while still applying pressure and building value.

Call 600. The pot is 1,800 so calling 600 gives ~25% pot odds — cheap enough to continue heads‑up. I have bottom pair (3♠) with a weak kicker; it's a marginal holding but has some showdown value versus a wide button c‑betting range. Flash's line (preflop raise, small flop c‑bet, turn bet) contains plenty of Qx/7x/5x and bluffs; raising as a bluff is too risky here (no useful blockers and behind most value hands). By calling I keep the pot under control, realize my equity, and preserve the ability to fold to a large shove on the river or call small river bets if the card is harmless. If I improve (trips) or he checks the river I can seek thin value; otherwise I will fold to heavy aggression.

Check. I have a marginal hand (bottom pair, 3♠ with a weak 6 kicker) on Q‑3‑7‑5‑9. Flash’s line (preflop raise, small flop c‑bet, turn action then a river check) represents a wide mix of Qx/7x/5x/9x and bluffs; many of those value hands still beat me and a small river bet will rarely get called by enough worse hands to be +EV. I have no meaningful blockers to bluff credibly and risking a bet just folds out a lot of air while getting called by better. With a 2,400 pot and deep effective stacks, lowest‑variance line is to check and take showdown value against bluffs/missed hands; if Flash now bets big I can fold and preserve chips, so I’ll check to see the showdown.

I have top pair with a Queen, but my kicker (a deuce) is the weakest possible. The board is relatively safe as the heart flush draw missed and there are no obvious straights. Minni has checked to me on the river after calling bets on the flop and turn. While I might have the best hand against her range (which includes missed draws or middle pairs like a 7), betting for value is thin because I am unlikely to be called by many worse hands, and I will almost certainly be beat by any other Queen. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot against her missed draws and marginal hands without risking a check-raise.

Heads-up on the button (I act first preflop but have position postflop) with J♣5♦ — a marginal but perfectly playable hand in heads-up. Opening a wide range from the button is standard: I want initiative and fold equity while keeping the pot reasonable. Min-raise to 200 (2x BB) accomplishes that — it applies pressure to the BB, preserves stack depth, and gives me a clear plan postflop (c-bet favorable low/dry flops, proceed cautiously on A/K-heavy boards). If Flash 3-bets big, I’ll fold preflop with this hand; if he calls I’ll use position to realize equity and probe with continuation bets when appropriate.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Eight suited is a premium hand. Facing a minimum raise from the button, I will 3-bet to build the pot and take control of the hand. A raise to 600 (3x the opponent's raise) is a standard value-building play that leverages my hand strength against a wide button opening range.

Heads-up on the button I opened wide to 200 with J♣5♦ to take initiative. Flash’s 3-bet to 600 is a standard, not huge, 3-bet size in this spot. With position and deep effective stacks, folding here is too nitty and a 4‑bet would be overspewing with a weak hand that lacks fold equity or strong blockers. Calling gives me good pot odds (800 in pot, 400 to call → ~33% required equity) and allows me to play postflop in position. Plan: call, see a flop, c-bet small on low/dry flops where I can credibly represent a range, play cautiously on A/K/Broadway-heavy boards and fold to heavy turn pressure unless I improve.

On this flop of Ks Js Kd, my Ac 8c has no direct pair, but I do hold an overcard and a backdoor nut flush draw. The board is very heavy with broadway cards, which likely connects with Minni's calling range for my 3-bet. However, as the pre-flop aggressor on a paired board, a continuation bet is standard to maintain pressure and potentially fold out middle-low pairs or air. I will place a small exploratory bet of 400, which is one-third of the pot, to see how Minni reacts while keeping the pot manageable if I am behind.

Flop K♠ J♠ K♦ — I hold J♣5♦: that’s two pair (Kings & Jacks). Flash was the preflop 3-bettor, so his range contains a good number of Ks that now have trips, but his flop stab is very small (400 into a 1600 pot) and can include a lot of bluffs and thin value. Raising here is thin: it bloats the pot and mostly gets called by hands that beat me (any K or KJ) while folding out some weaker hands I want to keep. Calling is the best line — I keep the pot manageable, allow worse hands and bluffs to continue, and use my positional advantage to make the turn decision with more information. Plan: call the 400 to see the turn cheaply; if he gives a clear turn shove or a large, polarized raise I’ll strongly weight folding to trips-heavy lines, but I’ll extract value or call small/medium bets against non-K turn cards.

The Ace on the turn gives me two pair (Aces and Kings), but the board is extremely coordinated. My opponent called a bet on a K-J-K flop, which strongly suggests they hold a King, a Jack, or a straight draw like Q-T. If they have a King, I am far behind a full house. If they have Q-T, they have a straight. By checking, I can gauge Minni's strength and control the pot size. I am likely ahead of a Jack, but behind a King or a straight. Checking is the most prudent move to avoid over-committing to a potentially second-best hand.

I have J♣5♦ on K♠ J♠ K♦ A♦ — my best five is K K J J A (two pair, Kings and Jacks). That’s a strong hand but extremely vulnerable: Flash 3‑betted preflop and continued on the flop, so his range contains many Ks and As (and pocket pairs like JJ) that currently beat me. He checked the turn after the A — a pot‑control/check‑back line that can come from both strong (A or slowplayed K) and weak hands, so a bet here risks bloating the pot and getting raised or jammed by almost all hands that beat me. With a 20.1k effective stack advantage and only 2 players left, I should control the pot size and see a river card in position. Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the river — call small/medium river bets for value, fold to a large polarized shove that represents trips/full house.

On this board of Ks-Js-Kd-Ad-7c, I have two pair, Aces and Kings with a Jack kicker (A-A-K-K-J). The turn check-check suggests that Minni may not have a King or the straight (Q-T), as those hands would often bet for value after the pre-flop aggressor checks. However, she called the flop, which contained two Kings and a Jack, indicating she likely has a piece of the board. If she has an Ace, we likely chop the pot. If she has a Jack or a pocket pair, I am currently ahead. If she was slow-playing a King or has the straight, I am behind. There is little value in betting here, as hands I beat (like a Jack) will likely fold to a river bet on such a scary board, and hands that beat me (a King or Q-T) will certainly call or raise. Checking is the most stable play to reach showdown and either win a modest pot or chop with another Ace.

I have J♣5♦ on K♠ J♠ K♦ A♦ 7♣ — that’s two pair (Kings and Jacks). However Flash 3‑bet preflop and led the flop, so his continuing range is heavy with Ks, As and big pairs that all beat me (any K, any A, QQ+; Kx/AK/ KJ combos produce full house/trips; JJ/KK/QQ also beat or tie). His small flop stab then check on the Ace turn and check on the river is a pot‑control line that can come from both strong made hands (slowplayed K/KK/AK) and marginal/holding hands. Betting here risks getting check‑raised or jammed by most hands that beat me while only getting value from a narrow slice of worse hands. With a 2,000‑chip pot and a 9,900‑chip opponent stack, I prefer to keep the pot controlled and take showdown rather than inflate a marginal spot. Plan: check to showdown; if Flash bets a small amount I’ll call for thin value, but I’ll fold to a large polarized raise/shove that represents trips/full house.

I am on the button with QsJs, a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, has a significantly larger stack, but I have a strong enough hand to raise for value and to seize initiative. Raising is standard here to build a pot with a hand that plays very well post-flop due to its high-card value and potential for straights and flushes.

Flash (SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with T♥6♥ — a marginal but suited hand that plays well heads-up. Calling 200 more (300 total committed including my 100 BB) is a small price to see a flop given the wide SB opening: I get ~33% immediate pot odds and retain flexibility post‑flop. I don’t want to 3‑bet light out of position and bloat the pot with a hand that prefers to see a flop, so I’ll call and evaluate on the flop.

Heads‑up vs a wide SB opener. I’m on the BB with T♥6♥ and the flop is 8♠7♠2♣ — relatively dry but with two spades. My hand has no pair but it has some equity: a gutshot to a 9 (completes 6-7-8-9-10) and backdoor heart/runner possibilities. Against a wide opening range many hands missed this flop, so a modest c-bet extracts folds and charges spade draws while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll make a half‑pot continuation bet (300) to apply pressure; if Flash raises big I’ll fold this marginal hand, if he calls I can proceed cautiously on the turn.

I have a strong flush draw with two overcards (Queen and Jack) on a board of 8s-7s-2c. Minni has led into me with a 'donk bet' of 300. Given that I have significant equity (approximately 45-50% against most of her range) and position, calling is a very solid option. It allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card cheaply without bloating the pot or facing a re-raise. I can use my position to evaluate her action on the turn.

I turned a pair: T♥6♥ on 8♠7♠2♣6♣. I bet the flop and was called, so Flash’s calling range is wide (spade draws, overcards, some small pairs). My 6 is a modest but real improvement — ahead of missed overcards and many draws but vulnerable to sets/8x/7x. I want to charge spade draws and extract value from hands like Kx/Qx/Tx and weaker pairs, while still keeping stack flexibility versus a raise. A bet of ~2/3 pot (800 into 1200) accomplishes that: it prices spade draws to pay to see a river and gets value from worse hands. If Flash check‑raises big I’ll seriously re‑evaluate (often folding to a large raise), but for now I’ll bet for value and protection.

On a turn card of 6c, the board is now 8s-7s-2c-6c. I have the Queen-high flush draw with the QsJs and two overcards. Minni has followed up her flop lead with a substantial bet of 800 into a 1,200 pot. My pot odds for the call are 800 to win a total pot of 2,800, which is approximately 28.5%. With 9 spade outs and potentially 6 overcard outs (Queens and Jacks), I have roughly a 30% chance to improve to what is likely the best hand on the river. Given that I have position and the direct odds to see the final card, calling is the most profitable play. Raising here would likely only be called by better hands (like two-pair or sets) and would force me to commit too many chips with a hand that still needs to improve.

I have T♥6♥ and ended up with only a pair of sixes. Flash opened wide and called both my flop and turn bets, so his continuing range is likely medium-strength (8x, 7x, some pocket pairs, and occasional sticky broadways) — many of those hands either already beat me or will continue to beat a river bet. The river 7♦ pairs the board and further reduces the relative value of my marginal pair (it creates trips/full-house possibilities and improves some parts of his range). Betting here would mostly get called by hands that beat me and will fold out very few worse hands, so it’s poor thin-value EV. I’ll check to control the pot and induce a bluff or a small river stab I can call. Plan: check; call small river bets (roughly ≤ ~1,200 chips) to catch bluffs or weaker pairs, but fold to large river bets/raises that represent cleared value (bigger than ~half‑pot/full pot), since my hand is often second-best.

The board has paired on the river (8s-7s-2c-6c-7d), and I have missed my flush draw and overcard improvement, leaving me with only Queen-high. Minni led out on both the flop and the turn but has now checked on the river. While her check could signal weakness or a missed draw (like 9-10 or 10-J), Queen-high has almost no showdown value against her range, which likely includes at least a pair or a better high card given her aggression on previous streets. However, at this stage of the tournament (hand 134 of 150) and with Minni holding a significant chip lead, a bluff here is risky as she can afford to call with any marginal hand or pair. Since Queen-high might occasionally win against total air (like 5-4 or a lower missed draw), and the risk of being called by a better hand is high, checking behind is the most prudent way to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range. K3o is a marginal hand but has a king-blocker and reasonable postflop playability in position — so I’ll apply standard heads-up aggression and raise to the min-open (200) to pick up the blinds or isolate Flash. The raise is small relative to both stacks and gives me fold equity; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand, if he calls I’ll use position to continuation-bet on dry boards and proceed cautiously on coordinated/draw-heavy flops.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 4h. Minni, who has a significant chip lead and has been playing very aggressively from the button, has raised to 200. Although I am getting 3:1 odds to call, 94 offsuit is an extremely weak hand to play out of position against a dominant opponent. Folding here preserves my stack for better spots where I can realize equity more effectively.

I am on the button with 2c 4s, which is one of the weakest hands in heads-up play. Even with the discounted price of 50 chips to call from the small blind, this hand has very little post-flop playability and is dominated by almost everything in Minni's range. Given that Minni has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively, there is no reason to put more chips in the pot with such a weak holding. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 10,000+ chip stack.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with Q5o — marginal but playable and much stronger when I have position. The pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to complete; rather than limp I want to take the initiative, apply pressure and deny Flash cheap looks. I’ll open to 3x the BB (300 total) — a size large enough to fold out very weak defenses but small enough to keep the pot controllable with a marginal hand. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (I’m not calling big preflop 3-bets with Q5o), if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops (Q/5 or dynamic draw boards) and play cautiously on dry, non-pairing boards, using position to control pot size.

I am in the Big Blind with 3-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. Given the extremely low strength of my hand and the fact that I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position against a player with a significant chip lead, a fold is the only prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities in the final hands of the tournament.
I am on the button with a suited hand (Jh 6h), which is well worth a raise in heads-up play. Establishing initiative and playing in position is key here.

Pocket Aces in the big blind vs a single button open is the best spot to be aggressive. I want to 3-bet for value and to isolate Flash — a raise to 1000 (≈3x the open) is a standard heads-up sizing: it builds the pot, reduces the number of speculative hands that can see a cheap flop, and gets called by worse hands (broadways, pocket pairs, suited connectors). I have a much larger stack but don’t need to jam; keep the line playable postflop and be prepared to call/shove against a shove given my equity.

In heads-up play, Jh6h is a playable hand, especially when in position. Minni's 3-bet to 1,000 requires me to call 700 more into a pot that will be 2,000, giving me 2.85:1 odds. Given my positional advantage on the button and the playability of suited connectors/gappers, calling is the most balanced move. Folding would be too tight, and 4-betting is unnecessary risk against the tournament chip leader.

I have pocket Aces (Ad As) on a 6d-9c-8d flop heads-up after 3-betting preflop. This is an overpair to the board and my Ad gives me the nut diamond blocker — I’m ahead of most of Flash’s calling range (broadways, weaker aces, medium pocket pairs, suited connectors) but there are some danger hands (flopped sets: 66/88/99 and two-card straights/strong combos). Betting is the +EV line: I want value and to charge/deny equity to diamond and straight draws while building the pot with the best hand. Against his stack I can size to extract value without overcommitting; a ~3/4-pot bet pressures draws and gets called by worse pairs and broadways. I’ll bet 1500 into the 2000 pot. If he raises big or shoves I’ll re-evaluate (call or shove depending on sizing) since AA is still a favorite vs his calling/raising range but I’ll proceed carefully versus very strong resistance (check-raise/shove).

On this highly coordinated flop of 6d-9c-8d, my bottom pair of sixes is extremely vulnerable. Minni's pre-flop 3-bet followed by a large 75% pot lead on the flop indicates significant strength, likely a superior pair (TT-AA), a nine, or a strong draw that has high equity against me. Given the board texture, almost any turn card (7, 10, 5, or any diamond) creates additional threats or completes straights. Calling 1,500 with only bottom pair in a 3-bet pot against the chip leader is an unnecessary risk. I will fold here to preserve my remaining 8,250 chips for more favorable spots in the final stages of the tournament.

Heads-up on the button (I’m also SB) with 7♣9♠ — this hand is playable and much stronger than in a full ring because of position. I want to seize the initiative, steal the BB and play the flop in position rather than limp and give a free card. Standard open-size here in this game has been ~300, and Flash has a ~91BB stack so a 300 open keeps pressure while preserving stack depth. Plan: open-raise to 300; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play aggressively in position; if he 3-bets big I’ll fold this marginal holding unless the sizing warrants a call.

I am currently in the Big Blind with Ace-Five suited, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni has been quite active as the button/small blind. While a call is standard given the pot odds, I want to take the initiative and 3-bet to put pressure on Minni and potentially win the pot pre-flop. With only a few hands left in the tournament and being significantly behind in chips, I need to capitalize on premium or semi-premium holdings to build my stack.

I opened to 300 on the button with 7♣9♠ and Flash 3-bet to 1000 (pot 1300, +700 to call). 7-9 offsuit is a marginal hand in a 3-bet pot — it's often dominated by a typical 3-bet range (pairs and broadway/Ax) and plays poorly multi-street out of position vs aggression. Flash still has ~82BB effective; with a big chip lead I don't need to gamble or explore a marginal 4‑bet/call spot. My earlier plan was to fold this holding to a big 3‑bet, so I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better opportunity.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Ten offsuit (As Th) is a premium hand. Being on the Button (Small Blind), I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is the most effective move here to build the pot and take control of the action. Folding is not an option given the strength of the hand, and calling is unnecessarily passive.

Heads-up against the button who opened to 300. Q♠4♥ is a weak, marginal hand — not something I want to 3‑bet with — but in heads‑up play the defending range from the big blind is very wide. The pot is 400 and I need to fold another 200 to call (total commitment from my seat 300), so I’m getting roughly 33% equity to continue. Flash opens frequently and likely has a fairly wide range here, so calling to realize equity and play the flop is the best option. Calling keeps the pot controllable, preserves my stack advantage (I can pressure later), and allows me to play postflop position/initiative decisions based on the board. If I face a large 3‑bet or heavy aggression postflop on an obviously unfavorable board, I’ll fold; if I hit a queen/4 or get good draws I’ll play aggressively for value. I’ll therefore call the raise and evaluate on the flop.

Flop: 8c Jh 4d — I hold Qs4h, which is a single pair (pair of 4s) with a decent kicker but a vulnerable hand heads‑up. Flash opened wide from the button, so his range contains lots of overcards, Jx/8x, and bluffs. Acting first, I don't want to inflate the pot with a marginal made hand that is often behind. Checking keeps the pot controllable, lets him bluff or c‑bet with worse, and preserves our ability to call small/medium bets or fold to heavy pressure. Plan: check and evaluate his action — call small-to-medium c-bets (~≤1/2 pot) to realize equity, fold to large turns/river shove or very large bets, and play aggressively only if I improve or get a clear read.

Holding Ace-high with a Ten kicker on a Jack-high board, I have a hand with some showdown value but limited strength. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni has checked to me on the flop, a continuation bet is the standard move. This applies pressure on her range and allows me to take down the pot if she missed. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard size for this texture.

Board 8c Jh 4d — I hold Qs4h: bottom pair with a weak kicker, out of position. I checked as planned and Flash's 300 c‑bet into the 900 pot is a small, standard size.
