Calling 300 into this pot gives ~25% pot odds (300/(900+300)=25%), and against a wide button c‑betting range my hand has better than that equity and realistic chances to improve. Raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and folding is too tight for the price. With a big stack advantage I’ll call to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn: fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I don’t improve, but value bet/raise if I pair my Queen, make two pair+, or pick up a clear read.

Check. I have second pair (4s) on 8♣-J♥-4♦-5♣ — a marginal, vulnerable hand out of position. Flash opened wide and c‑bet the flop, so his range contains many Jx/8x/overcards and also hands that already beat me (8x, Jx, 5x, etc.). The 5 on the turn also completes some straight combos (6‑7) and makes several hands that were behind now beat me. Betting here would bloat the pot with a hand that is often behind and is vulnerable to a raise; a small “block” bet gains little and is easily exploited by aggression. By checking I keep the pot controlled (pot = 1200), allow bluffs/overcards to barrel, and can call small turn bets to realize equity while folding to heavy pressure. Plan: check — if Flash checks back we get to showdown cheaply; if he bets small I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the river; if he bets big or shoves I’ll fold.

On the turn card 5c, the board texture remains relatively dry. My Ace-Ten high has some showdown value, and after Minni checks for a second time, checking back is a safe play to control the pot size and see a free river card. Betting again would essentially turn my hand into a bluff, and given Minni's stack lead and tendency to call, I prefer to keep the pot manageable and hope to realize my equity or catch a pair on the river.

I hold Q♠4♥ and the river gives me trips (4h + 4d + 4s) on 8c‑Jh‑4d‑5c‑4s — a very strong hand heads‑up: almost always good (only full houses/quads beat me: pocket JJ/88/55 or the last 4). Flash checked the turn and checked back the river, which looks like weakness or a medium hand rather than a slow‑played full house. With the pot only 1,200 and a wide button range, I want to extract value from Jx, 8x, 5x and other worse pairs/second‑pairs that will call a reasonable river bet. I’ll make a value bet rather than check and give a free showdown. I size it to ~2/3 pot (800) — big enough to earn value from worse hands but not so large as to fold out most callers or induce an immediate fold. If Flash raises big/shoves, I’ll re‑evaluate (those raises are likely full houses/quads or rare bluffs), but given stack sizes and how rarely he has the locked full house/quads, a modest value bet here maximizes expected value.

On a board of 8-J-4-5-4, I am holding Ace-Ten high. Pre-flop, I raised and Minni called. On the flop, I continuation-bet 300 and Minni called, which indicates she likely has a piece of the board or a draw. The turn was checked through, showing weakness on my part. On the river, the board pairs with a 4, and Minni leads out for 800 into a 1200 pot. This 'donk' lead after check-calling the flop and checking the turn is often indicative of a medium-strength hand (like an 8 or a small pocket pair) looking for value from Ace-high, or a slow-played Jack. While there is a small chance she is bluffing with a missed straight draw like 9-10 or 6-7, my Ace-high is merely a bluff-catcher here. Given my stack size and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament, I prefer to fold and preserve my chips rather than calling a significant bet with only Ace-high against a range that likely connected with the board.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) gives me a large postflop advantage, so I should be opening a wide range. Q♣2♥ is marginal but fine to raise here to steal the BB and take the initiative. I’ll make a standard heads-up open to 300 total (adds 200 more to the pot), which is small relative to my 31k stack and gives good fold equity. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot; if he 3-bets large I’ll let this one go — Q2o plays poorly vs heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 6h, which is a strong suited connector hand in heads-up play. Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 3BB (300). Calling 200 more to see a flop with a hand that has good potential to hit straights or flushes is a standard and profitable play. I have a healthy enough stack to navigate post-flop, and I want to keep the pot manageable while looking for a favorable board.

On a flop of Jc-9h-Tc, my hand (5h 6h) has missed completely. This board is very coordinated and favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which likely includes many high cards, pairs, and straight draws. With only 6-high and a very weak backdoor flush draw, I have no reason to lead into the chip leader. I will check and most likely fold to any significant bet.

Heads-up as the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet a wide range on this flop. Board J♣–9♥–T♣ is highly connected, but Flash checked, so I can take the initiative and fold out a lot of unpaired hands and thin equity. My Q♣2♥ gives me a useful overcard plus a club (backdoor flush possibility) and the ability to make a straight if a K or 8 comes (8 immediate outs to improve by the turn). I don’t want to allow cheap equity to draws, so I’ll size up to put real pressure but keep the pot manageable — about 2/3 pot. Plan: bet to take it down now; if Flash raises big I’ll give up (Q2o plays poorly vs strong aggression); if called I’ll proceed cautiously and only continue when I improve or pick up additional info.

On a board of Jc-9h-Tc, my 6-high (5h 6h) has virtually no equity. This flop is extremely coordinated and favors the pre-flop raiser's range, likely containing many Jacks, Tens, Nines, or straight draws (KQ, Q8, etc.). With no direct draws and a small bet to call into a dangerous board, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

In heads-up play, 9-7 offsuit is a standard hand to open from the button. I have 86 big blinds, which gives me plenty of room to play post-flop. I will maintain my consistent opening size of 300 to keep pressure on the big blind and maintain my table image.

Heads-up in the big blind with pocket tens vs a standard button open — this is a clear 3-bet-for-value spot. Flash's open to 300 looks like a reasonably wide heads-up opening; TT is ahead of a lot of that range (broadways, many suited aces, weaker pairs, suited connectors). I want to isolate and build the pot while I still have plenty of depth vs Flash (he has ~8,350), so I’ll make a standard-sized 3-bet that gets value and charges draws but doesn’t commit me. I’ll raise to 1,000 total: if he folds we pick up the pot, if he calls we play a sizable pot postflop with a strong pocket pair; I’ll re-evaluate vs any large 4-bet shove.

Minni has shown significant aggression recently, and 9-7 offsuit is a weak hand to defend against a 3-bet. While I am on the button, calling 700 more with a hand that has poor post-flop playability in a bloated pot is not ideal given my stack size relative to hers. I will fold and wait for a better spot to contest a pot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Q♦6♣. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to call, but heads-up the button should be opening very wide. Q6o has decent playability in position and I want fold equity and initiative versus Flash. Min-raising to 200 is allowed but is too small — I’ll open to a standard 3xBB (300 total) to discourage limp/calls and to build a pot I can play in position. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll strongly consider folding Q6o; if he calls I can use position to steer the hand postflop.

I have been dealt pocket Jacks (Jc Jd) in the Big Blind. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With 8250 chips remaining and a strong premium hand, I need to protect my equity and build the pot. Minni has been very active, and while they have a significant chip lead, a 3-bet is the standard and most profitable play here to either take the pot down now or go to a flop with a range advantage and the initiative. I will raise to 1000.

I opened to 300 with Q♦6♣ and Flash 3‑bet to 1000. Calling 700 into a 1300 pot would require ~35% equity to be correct; Q6o is frequently dominated by a 3‑betting BB range and is a poor candidate to 4‑bet bluff (min 4‑bet to 1700 is too small to fold him out, shove is too loose). I planned to fold to a big 3‑bet — preserve my stack and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

I am on the button with a weak hand (9d 4c). With only a few hands left in the tournament and being significantly out-chipped by Minni, there is little incentive to play a weak hand out of position post-flop unless I am trying to steal. However, Minni has been active and defending well. I will fold to preserve my remaining 8600 chips and look for a better spot in the final few hands.

Heads-up on the button A8o is a strong raising hand — I want to take the initiative and avoid letting the BB see a cheap flop. I’ll open-raise rather than just call the extra 50 to build the pot and generate fold equity. 300 is a standard, table-consistent sizing (about 3x BB) that keeps my range wide and lets me play postflop in position; if Flash 3-bets I can re-evaluate given his stack. Amount is the total I’ll have committed preflop (including my posted 50).

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit, a strong hand in heads-up play. The tournament is nearing its end (Hand 145/150) and I am significantly behind in chips. Minni has been opening frequently from the button. In recent hands (like #139 and #142), 3-betting to 1000 has been effective in picking up the pot pre-flop. I will raise to 1000 to take the initiative and either win the pot now or set up a strong position for the flop.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♣8♥ and Flash 3‑bet to 1000. The pot is 1300 and I need to call 700 (total committed 1000). Heads‑up A8o is a very playable hand in position — it has good equity and postflop playability against a 3‑betting range. Effective stack (opponent) is ~76bb, so a shove/large 4‑bet here would be too polarizing and often put me in big pots dominated by stronger hands. Folding is too nitty. I’ll call the 700 (commit total 1000) to see a flop in position, plan to lead when I hit an Ace/8 or strong draws, and exercise pot control or fold to heavy resistance on dry boards.

On a very coordinated board of Kd-Qh-Jh, my Ace-Nine offsuit has missed, although I do have a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (needing a Ten). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet, this board is quite dangerous as it hits a significant portion of Minni's calling range (pocket pairs, broadway cards). I will check to see how Minni reacts and to control the size of the pot, given that I am currently likely behind any piece of this board.

I missed this flop with A♣8♥ — no pair and only a very weak backdoor heart and a backdoor Broadway (need a T). Flash’s preflop 3‑bet skews his range toward hands that connect strongly with K‑Q‑J (Kx/Qx/Jx, broadways, T9), and his flop check looks like pot‑control or a trap rather than a clear fold. Betting here would bloat the pot and often force me into tough decisions vs better hands or a check‑raise; in position I prefer to control the pot and gather more information. Plan: check to see the turn and only become aggressive if I hit an A/8/heart or a Ten (or the opponent shows clear weakness); otherwise be ready to fold to significant turn aggression.

The turn card, the Three of Diamonds, is a complete blank that does not significantly change the texture of the board. My current hand remains Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). Since the flop went check-check, it is possible that Minni has a weak hand, but this specific board (K-Q-J) is very dangerous as it likely hits many hands in a button-calling range (pairs and broadway cards). I will check again to keep the pot manageable and try to see a free or cheap river card. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate the price based on my four outs to the nut straight and my remaining stack, but for now, checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control.

Check. I missed this board with A♣8♥ — I have only Ace‑high and very limited improvement potential: 4 tens + 3 aces = 7 outs (~15% to improve on the river). The K♦ Q♥ J♥ 3♦ is extremely coordinated and connects well with Flash’s preflop 3‑betting range (Kx/Qx/Jx, broadways, sets, two‑pair), and his flop/turn checks can easily be either pot‑control with strong hands or a sign of weakness. Betting the turn would bloat the pot and often put me in tough spots facing calls or check‑raises from better hands; as a bluff it’s thin given the few credible turn cards that fold out his range. I prefer to keep the pot small, see the river for one more card, and then re‑evaluate: if I pair the A or hit a T I can pursue value; if the river blanks and he bets, I’ll fold unless I pick up a very clear read. Pot control and information are the priority here, so I check to the river.

On a river of 4s, the board is Kd-Qh-Jh-3d-4s. My Ace-Nine offsuit is only Ace-high. This board texture (K-Q-J) is very favorable for a button-calling range, as it contains many broadway cards and pairs. I was the pre-flop aggressor but checked the flop and turn for pot control. Checking the river is the most sensible play here; if Minni checks back, I might occasionally win against a lower Ace or a complete bluff, and if she bets, I can evaluate based on her sizing. Betting myself is unlikely to force a fold from a King, Queen, or Jack, which are all very likely to be in her range.

I check. Holding A♣8♥ on K♦ Q♥ J♥ 3♦ 4♠ I only have Ace‑high and almost no showdown value versus a preflop 3‑better’s range. Flash’s 3‑bet preflop plus passive check‑check line is consistent with many hands that beat me (Kx/Qx/Jx, two‑pair/sets, strong broadways) and many of those will not fold to a river bluff. Fold equity is low and the pot (2,000) is small relative to both stacks, so betting risks chips for little expected gain. Pot control and stack preservation are the priority — check to showdown and fold to any meaningful river aggression if it occurs.

I am on the Button/Small Blind with 8d 9s. With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and a significant stack deficit relative to Minni, I need to remain active to try and close the gap. 98o is a solid hand to open with in heads-up play to exert pressure on the Big Blind and maintain initiative. I will raise to 300, which is consistent with my standard opening size in previous hands.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with 7c6s — a playable hand heads‑up. Calling 200 more (300 total) is cheap given pot odds (risk 200 to win ~600, need ~25% equity) and the hand has decent flop playability. I’ll call to see a flop, continue with pairs/strong draws or use position/stack advantage to apply pressure later, and fold to heavy unambiguous aggression when missed.

I have 7c6s on 6c-Ac-Js: only bottom pair (6s) with a very weak backdoor club possibility. Flash was the preflop raiser on the button so his range includes lots of Ax and broadway hands that dominate me on this A‑high flop. Out of position with a marginal made hand, I want pot control and information — don’t fire as an OOP bluff. I’ll check to keep the pot small and react to his action: call small/medium continuation bets to realize equity, fold to clear strong aggression or large raises, and reassess on the turn if checked to or called.

On an Ace-high board like 6c Ac Js, as the pre-flop raiser, I have a range advantage. This board hits many of the hands I would raise with (AX, KX, QJ, etc.). Minni's check provides an opportunity to continuation bet and potentially take the pot down immediately if she didn't connect. A bet of 300 (half-pot) is a standard size to test her strength without overcommitting with 9-high.

Flash (button) c-bet small (300) on an A‑high flop. I’ve got bottom pair (6s) with one club — weak but with some showdown value and a backdoor club/runner straight potential. Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives ~25% break‑even equity; against a standard c‑bet range I have enough equity to justify a call rather than folding or bluff‑raising out of position. Plan: call to keep the pot controlled, see the turn, fold to clear heavy turn aggression unless I improve (two pair/trips) or the turn is a harmless blank and opponent slows down.

I have 7c6s — just a pair of sixes (6s) with a marginal 7 kicker. The board is 6c Ac Js 4c: Ace‑high and now three clubs on the board; I do NOT have a flush (only one club in my hand). Flash was the preflop raiser and c‑betted the flop, but the turn checked through (to me) which looks like weakness or pot control from a range that contains lots of Ax, Js and broadway hands that currently beat me. Out of position with a vulnerable one‑pair hand, I want pot control and to avoid getting blown off by a river shove — checking preserves chips and lets him show intent. Plan: check and call a small turn/river probe but fold to clear heavy aggression unless I improve. Checking keeps my range reasonable and avoids committing with a marginal holding.

Minni called my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board, which strongly suggests she holds an Ace, a Jack, or a draw. The 4 of clubs on the turn completes potential flush draws and is generally a better card for the defender's range. With 9-high and no clubs in my hand, I have almost zero equity and bluffing into a range that called an Ace-high flop is risky. I will check back to see a free river and control the pot size, preserving my stack for the final few hands of the tournament.

I check. I have a weak one‑pair (pair of sixes) on 6c Ac Js 4c Jh. Flash was the preflop raiser and c‑betted the flop (I called) but checked the turn and river, which shows weakness/pot control — but his range still contains many hands that beat me (Ax, Jx, some broadways that chop/beat me). I have very little fold equity and a bet would either get called by better hands or only fold the absolute air; I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal holding out of position. With deep stacks and only a small pot (1200), checking to showdown preserves chips and realizes my showdown value against bluffs/missed hands.

On the river, the board has paired the Jack (Js, Jh) and contains an Ace. I have 9-high with no showdown value. Minni called my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board, which typically indicates she has a pair of Aces, a Jack, or a pocket pair. While her checks on the turn and river suggest a lack of extreme strength, they also signify a willingness to go to showdown with a marginal hand. Attempting to bluff her off an Ace or a Jack is statistically unlikely to succeed, and with only a few hands left in the tournament and a significant stack disadvantage, I must preserve my chips rather than risking them on a low-probability bluff. I will check back and concede the pot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind), I should be opening very wide. 7s9h is a playable, connected hand with good postflop playability, so I want the initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (3× BB) — big enough to generate fold equity against a wide BB defense but small enough to keep weaker hands in and preserve deep-stack flexibility. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold or re-evaluate; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and play for value/pressure postflop.

With only 4 hands left in the tournament and my stack at 8,900 compared to Minni's 30,700, I need to be selective but also defensive. Holding Queen-Five offsuit in the big blind against a standard button raise of 300, I am getting 2:1 odds to call. While Q5o is a marginal hand, in heads-up play, it has decent equity against a wide button opening range. Given the stage of the tournament and the pot odds, I will call the additional 200 to see a flop and look for opportunities to outplay the opponent or hit a pair, rather than folding and losing my blind without a fight.

On a flop of 9-2-3, my Queen-high hand hasn't connected with any part of the board. As the big blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I will see if Minni bets and evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, I want to keep the pot small given the weakness of my hand.

Flop: 9♦ 2♦ 3♠. I have 9♥7♠ — top pair (nines) with a decent kicker in a heads-up spot vs a very wide BB calling range. This is a clear c-bet spot: the board is mostly dry except for diamond flush draws, so I want to charge draws and get value from worse pairs, guts/weak made hands and floats. Size ~2/3 pot (400 into 600) balances getting value and denying equity to diamond hands while keeping the pot manageable vs his 8.7k stack. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I will proceed cautiously on diamond turns (pot-control/check/raise depending on action) and fire again on safe cards; if he check-raises heavily I’ll give credit to a strong made hand and fold to very large overbets, but call smaller/medium raises to realize equity.

On a flop of 9-2-3, I have missed completely with my Queen-high hand. Minni has made a continuation bet of 400 into a 600-chip pot (two-thirds pot). While I have two overcards to the low cards and one overcard to the nine, Queen-Five offsuit is extremely weak and lacks any significant draw or backdoor potential. Continuing out of position against a much larger stack with only 4 hands left in the tournament is unnecessarily risky. I will fold and preserve my 8,400 chips to look for a more advantageous situation in the final three hands.

With 7s6d on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a standard opening hand. My stack of 8650 is relatively short compared to Minni's 31200, so I want to maintain aggression but avoid overcommitting. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand pre-flop.

Heads-up, I'm in the big blind facing a 300 open from the button. I have A♦3♥ — a weak ace but one that fares well against a wide button opening range. The pot is 400 and I need to add 200 more (300 total committed including my posted 100) so my break-even equity is about 33%. Versus a wide SB open my hand typically has that equity and can win big when I hit an ace or improve on the board. I’m also a big chip leader (31.2k vs his 8.4k) late in the match, so I want to avoid bloating the pot out-of-position with a marginal kicker; a small 3-bet won’t gain much and a large 3-bet creates unnecessary variance. I’ll call, see a flop, and proceed cautiously OOP — extract value when I hit and fold to strong aggression when I miss.

Flop: 4c-6h-7d. I have Ad-3h — just Ace-high with a single gutshot to a 5 (4 outs). That’s only ~8.7% to hit on the turn (~16% by river), so I have limited equity and no flush/clean two-card draw. Flash checked to me, and I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot, realize my (small) equity cheaply, and fold to substantial aggression. If he fires a small c-bet I can call to see the turn; I’ll give up to big bets or strong raises.

I have flopped top two pair (7s 6d) on a 4-6-7 board. This is a very strong hand, but the board is highly coordinated with straight draws (any 5 or 8). Minni checked to me, and I need to bet for value and to protect against those draws. A bet of 400 into a 600 pot is a standard sizing to extract value from pairs and draws while building a pot for my strong holding.

Flop: 4♣-6♥-7♦. I have A♦3♥ — just Ace-high with a single gutshot to a 5 (4 outs: ~8.7% to hit the turn, ~16% to river). Flash bet 400 into the pot (pot shows 1000), so calling 400 would require ≈28.6% equity to breakeven (400/(1000+400)). My raw draw equity is below that, but heads-up the button’s opening/c-bet range is wide and contains a lot of missed overcards and bluffs that my Ace can currently beat. I’m the big chip leader (31.2k vs ~8k), so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand by 3-betting. Calling is the least-variance, highest-expected-value line here: it lets me realize equity cheaply, keeps bluffs in, and allows me to fold to substantial turn aggression. I’ll call the 400 and re-evaluate on the turn (look to fold to large barrels, extract value if I improve or he shows weakness).

Check. I only have A3o — a marginal Ace-high with just three clean outs to a pair and no backdoor draw. I called the flop to realize equity cheaply; the King on the turn actually helps a lot of hands in Flash’s raising/c-betting range (Kx, some two-pair/sets, and strong pair+backdoor combos). I’m out of position and the pot is already 1,400; with a big chip lead late in the match I should control the pot size and avoid creating unnecessary variance by barreling or leading OOP with a weak kicker. Plan: check and see his action — call small turn bets (half‑pot or less) to reach the river and fold to larger pressure, or take the pot down if he checks behind and the river is harmless.

I have two pair (7s and 6d) on a board of 4c-6h-7d-Kc. The turn King of Clubs is an overcard, but it is unlikely to have improved Minni's hand given her flop call, unless she was floating with K-high. My hand remains very strong against her range, which likely includes one-pair hands like 7x or 6x, and straight draws like 8x or 5x. Since Minni checked to me again, I will place a value bet of 850 (roughly 60% of the pot) to extract value from those weaker hands and to protect against potential draws before the river.

Call 850. Heads-up vs a wide button range and with a big stack lead, I want to keep the pot under control and realize my showdown equity rather than bloat the pot OOP with A3. The turn bet (850 into a 2,250 pot) is small (<half‑pot) and will include a lot of bluffs / thin value hands I currently beat, so calling keeps those in. Pot odds for the call are ~27.4%; my naive river outs (3 Aces + 3 Threes + 4 Fives = 10 outs) give ~21.7% raw equity, but against a wide range my Ace‑high will still beat many missed hands, so a call is the low‑variance, +EV line. I’ll call the 850, fold to a large raise/shove, and re‑evaluate on the river (value bet or fold depending on showdown strength and opponent’s action).

Board: 4c-6h-7d-Kc-5h. I hold Ad-3h — only Ace-high. Flash has shown strength (preflop open, flop c-bet, turn barrel) so his range contains lots of made hands that now beat me (Kx, 7x, 6x, 5x and straights with 8 or remaining 3s/8s). The river 5 also completes some straights, and my thin blocker (one 3) isn’t enough to confidently turn this into a bluff or thin-value bet. With a big chip lead and no bet to call, the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and take showdown rather than bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand.

On the river board of 4c-6h-7d-Kc-5h, I hold two pair (sevens and sixes). The river 5h is a very dangerous card as it completes a four-card straight (4-5-6-7) on the board, meaning any hand containing an 8 or a 3 now has a straight. Minni has check-called me on the flop and turn, which is consistent with holding a draw or a mid-pair. While my two pair is a strong hand, betting here is risky; a better hand (like a straight) is unlikely to fold, and a worse hand (like a single pair of Kings or Sevens) might not call a third barrel on such a coordinated board. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and protect my remaining stack as we approach the end of the tournament.

Heads-up on the button (I’ve already posted the SB). Q♦3♦ is a playable, suited queen in heads-up — I want initiative and to pressure the big blind. A standard 3BB open works well here: it’s large enough to generate fold equity against Flash’s defending range but small enough to keep the pot controllable given his ~7050 stack. Plan: fold to a big 3-bet, call or stack off to light 3-bets only if sizing is small; if called, play postflop in position, c-betting on favorable textures and using the backdoor diamond equity and positional advantage. Raising to 300 maximizes my heads-up EV while protecting my deep stack.

In Hand #149 of a 150-hand match, I am dealt Ace-Jack offsuit in the big blind against a button raise from Minni. A-J is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has been very active from the button. I will put in a 3-bet to 1000 to extract value from her range and take control of the pot. With only two hands remaining in the tournament, I need to capitalize on strong starting hands to increase my final stack size.

Call to 1000 total. Heads-up on the button Q♦3♦ is worth defending — Flash’s 3-bet to 1000 (3.3x) is a standard, not huge sizing and likely contains a fairly wide range. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves my big stack, and lets me use position postflop to realize equity (backdoor diamonds + Q-high/straight potential) and apply pressure when favorable. I avoid 4-betting light (would create a marginal showdown or induce a shove I don’t want to face given opponent’s shorter stack). If Flash jams a larger 4-bet I’ll fold; if called I’ll c-bet or float depending on the flop texture and his tendencies.

On a very dry flop of 2-7-4 rainbow in a 3-bet pot, I hold the range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. My Ace-Jack is currently the best hand against much of Minni's calling range, which likely consists of broadway cards and small-to-medium pocket pairs. A continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot (800 chips) allows me to maintain initiative, fold out her air hands that have equity against me (like KQ, KJ, KT), and represent overpairs. Given there are only two hands left in the tournament, I want to protect my current lead in the hand and pick up the pot without seeing a turn.

Heads‑up on the button with Q♦3♦ on 2♣‑7♠‑4♦. I have position, a Q‑high and a backdoor diamond flush draw (two diamonds in my hand + 4♦ on board). Flash’s small flop stab (800 into a 2800 pot) looks like a probe/c‑bet and can be a fairly wide range heads‑up. Calling 800 into the 3600 pot gives ~22% break‑even equity — with position, backdoor equity and the chance to bluff or realize showdown, this hand is worth defending. Raising here is too thin (very little made or draw equity and it risks a shove from his shorter stack); folding is too tight given the price and positional advantage. I’ll call, keep the pot under control, and decide on the turn based on the card and his action (check‑call or barrel with equity when appropriate).

The deuce on the turn pairs the board, which is generally a blank card for both ranges, though it slightly favors the pre-flop caller if they have some low-frequency suited connectors with a deuce. Having 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, I have represented a strong range. However, Minni has called twice, indicating she likely has a pair (7x, 4x, or a pocket pair like 55/66). With only 5350 chips remaining and a pot of 3600, I am approaching a stack-to-pot ratio where I need to be careful. Checking here allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity with Ace-high, and potentially induce a bluff from her missed broadway floats or smaller pairs that might check behind for pot control. If she bets, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the most prudent way to reach the final hand of the tournament with a healthy stack.

Turn card (2♠) kills my backdoor diamond plan — with Q♦3♦ I now have no made hand and very little equity. Flash’s line (small flop stab, then check on the paired turn) is consistent with pot‑control or a trap, so barreling here would be a thin bluff into a player with a shortish stack who can call shoves/lightly. I’ll keep the pot under control and see the river: check and reevaluate based on his river action (look to bluff or value-bet only if I improve or he gives a clear fold/button). Checking avoids committing chips with essentially air and preserves maximum fold equity/room to maneuver on the river.

The river is an 8, completing a board of 2-7-4-2-8. My Ace-Jack has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot, especially after the turn went check-check. Minni's check on the turn suggests she is likely in pot-control mode with a weak pair (like 55, 66, or a 7) or has a missed broadway draw. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a better hand, and it's also unlikely to be called by a worse hand. By checking, I can realize my equity and win against her missed draws (KQ, QJ, KT, etc.) or potentially lose a small amount if she bets and I decide to fold or call. Given there is only one hand remaining in the tournament, preserving my stack for the final hand while trying to reach showdown cheaply is the optimal play.

River is 2♣-7♠-4♦-2♠-8♣ and I hold Q♦3♦ — I missed completely (no pair, no flush). Flash’s line (3-bet preflop, small flop stab, check turn, check river) can represent missed broadways but also real showdown/value hands (2x trips, 7x/4x/8x, medium pairs). The board is paired and any deuce, a 7/4/8, or many pocket pairs beat me; a river bluff risks a shove or a call from these hands for very thin fold equity. Pot is 3600 and Flash has ~5350 behind — I don’t want to create a marginal all‑in spot with essentially air. I’ll keep the pot under control and check to see showdown rather than bluffing a low‑equity river.

K9o is a strong hand in a heads-up scenario, ranking well above the average starting hand. As this is the final hand of the 150-hand session, I want to be aggressive to maximize my final stack size. A standard 3x raise to 300 from the small blind is a balanced and effective way to build the pot or take it down immediately.

2♣4♦ is one of the weakest hands to defend with. Facing a raise to 300 from the button, the pot is 400 and calling 200 requires ~33% equity to break even — far more than 2-4o has vs a reasonable button opening range. A small 3-bet is unlikely to fold enough hands and a shove risks a large portion of the stacks for negative EV. With the final hand and a big chip lead, preserving chips is the best play — fold.
